title: Venice prepares to close off streets and divert cruise ships
content:
The mayor Luigi Brugnaro spoke of a “black alert”, a term normally used to advise drivers against motorway travel on the days of most intense holiday traffic. Venice is preparing to cordon off its streets, set up diversions and one-way streets, and to move motorboats from St Mark’s to a less central area, to cope with the expected assault of tourists over the long May bank holiday weekend. A taste of the summer to come, with crowds, bottlenecked streets and queues at the vaporetto landing stages was already seen at Easter, which is why the council has decided to take action and experiment with interactive tourist flow management. “We want to defend and safeguard Venice,” explained Brugnaro. “It’s a commitment that we have entered into with UNESCO, but above all with the residents.” 
 
Reservations requiredThe mayor is asking for more extensive powers, which would allow him to cut off access to the historic centre on the busiest tourist days by introducing mandatory booking and passes. He however will need an ad hoc provision from the government, possibly to be included in the new special legislation for Venice, which the city authorities and business associations have been pressing for. In the meantime, they will have to manage the almost 30 million tourists who arrive each year with the existing laws. The ordinance signed a few days ago by the mayor to “make the historic city, safe, secure and liveable” is the first step: Brugnaro may be talking of experimentation, but he already has plans to implement the system throughout the summer. “Venice is fragile, and we will conduct trial runs, but regardless of what the complainers always say, we do not want to drive tourists away; if they respect the city they are welcome. The ‘black alert’, however, is a way of warning those who want to visit that there could be an extraordinarily high number of tourists over the coming days, which could make visiting the city difficult,” he said. Record numbers are expected for the May Day bank holiday weekend, with almost all hotels fully booked, and the number of tourists set to exceed the 200,000 of the Easter weekend. It is thus no coincidence that Brugnaro has asked day trippers to reschedule for other dates. However, from Saturday 28 April to Tuesday 1 May, measures are in place to divert pedestrian and water traffic, for example by closing the Ponte della Costituzione or restricting access to the Lista di Spagna, the street leading from the station directly to Rialto. The city council calls them “plunger blocks”, and emphasizes that their closure is only envisaged during times of heavy pedestrian traffic, and will only affect tourists. 
Passes for residentsResidents, workers and commuters, meanwhile, will be able to move around freely with the “Venezia unica” travel card. The wooden-clad motor launches arriving from the beaches and hinterland, which every day spew out hundreds of people at a time directly into St Mark’s Square, will be moved to Fondamenta Nuove, in a less central area of the old town, while if parking spaces run out at Piazzale Roma, the police will restrict traffic on the Ponte della Libertà. The latest news on possible detours will be posted on the Twitter and Facebook profiles of the local police and council; those who do not respect the provisions risk a fine of between €25 and €500.  English translation by Simon Tanner Article in Italian I PIÚ VISTI

title: Ryanair CEO: “More airlines in Europe will soon go bust. EasyJet will be broken up, Wizz Air has no future”
content:
Trump attacca la Ue: «Nazioni decadenti guidate da leader deboli». E paragona Zelensky all'imprenditore del circo P. T. Barnum Salva questo articolo e leggilo quando vuoi. Il servizio è dedicato agli utenti registrati. Trovi tutti gli articoli salvati nella tua area personale nella sezione preferiti e sull'app Corriere News. The head of Europe’s leading low cost carrier speaks: in Rome he pulls an aircraft because Fiumicino is too expensive, expands at airports that scrap the municipal tax ROME — The head of Ryanair expects that in the coming weeks and months more small and medium-sized airlines in Europe will go bankrupt, creating greater opportunities for the continent’s largest low-cost carrier. At the same time, he is trying to put serious pressure on Wizz Air in its main market (Central and Eastern Europe), calls on European Commission president Ursula von der Leyen to step down, and assures that even though from mid-November passengers will be required to check in online only via the airline’s app, “we certainly won’t turn away anyone who shows up at the boarding gate with a printed paper ticket.” Michael O’Leary, CEO of a group of carriers (Ryanair, Malta Air, Lauda, and Buzz), doesn’t care much for diplomacy: he removes an aircraft from Rome Fiumicino because in his view it’s too expensive, demands the lifting of movement limits at Ciampino, the capital’s other airport, and urges regions to follow the example of Friuli-Venezia Giulia, Calabria, and Sicily, which scrapped the municipal surcharge at airports, allowing the low-cost giant to add planes and flights there. “We have over a million seats we can add to Italy right away,” he says in a chat with Corriere della Sera on the sidelines of a press conference in Rome.    



 Let’s start with the past 24 hours: first the Icelandic carrier Play and then part of Sweden’s Braathens went bankrupt. Should Europe brace for more airlines ceasing operations?“Yes.” When?“I’d say in the coming months, as we get closer to winter. The problem in Europe is that there are many airlines pretending to be low-cost, but they have a high-cost structure. Play never made a profit. And there will be other small operators struggling in the market. There will be more bankruptcies before Christmas. I also think Wizz Air is in trouble.” Will it end up grounding planes?“I don’t think it will collapse in the coming weeks, but I think it’s inevitable that at some point it will either fail or be acquired by someone.” Wizz Air is restructuring its network, it even closed its Abu Dhabi base to only fly where it can profitably.“They’re focusing more on Eastern Europe.” You are also investing heavily there, in their market.“Oh no, those are our markets. Wizz Air doesn’t have one of its own.” I wonder if your fierce, direct competition is putting their future at risk.“No, they jeopardized their own future. During Covid they sold their aircraft and leased them back at high rates. In recent years, they expanded their fleet by buying jets from their main shareholder. Then they turned to a bank to refinance them with these costly sale-and-leaseback deals. Over the past five years, Wizz has booked profits from these transactions in their accounts, like some sort of Ponzi scheme. The more you grow, the more profit you make. But now growth has stopped, and they can’t do that anymore.” And now that’s hurting them?“They’re all over the place. Five years ago, they wanted to expand in Italy, then Vienna. And they vanished. Then they were supposed to become the number one airline in the Middle East. And they shut down their Abu Dhabi base because… there’s sand in the bloody desert. Quite the surprise for everyone. Now they want to go to Kazakhstan, grow again in Poland. But it’s too late to grow in Poland, because we’re already investing there. Overall, it looks like they won’t even make a single euro of profit. While we’ve got European airports lining up asking us to grow with them.” Why is that?“You know why? Because they quietly tell us, yes, Wizz flies from here, but they fear they’ll be gone next year.” So no low-cost carrier is safe?“Not even easyJet: it’s not in trouble, but it’s not growing. It only has a strong presence at London Gatwick, Paris Charles de Gaulle and Orly, and in Switzerland.” What will happen to it?“In the medium term, I think Air France-KLM will buy easyJet’s operations in Paris and Switzerland. And maybe British Airways will buy those at Gatwick.” In recent days there was a furious dispute between you and Aena, the Spanish operator managing almost all the country’s airports.“They accused us of lying. Which, coming from a former politician now running Aena, says it all.” They accuse you of distorting reality, of extortion.“A monopoly airport operator accusing a client of extortion. The situation in Spain is pretty simple. Aena’s view is: ‘it’s our monopoly, we run the airports.’ They basically charge the same per-passenger fee in Barcelona and Madrid as they do in regional airports like Valladolid, Jerez, Santiago. And Santiago and Valladolid are bloody empty, while everyone wants to go to Madrid, Barcelona, Malaga, and Palma.” And so?“They want us to grow in the regional airports. We said: ‘we can grow there, but you need to cut the fees.’ And they said: ‘no, we won’t cut them.’” So you nearly gave up on regional airports… “We had a three-year growth deal that got us into Valladolid, Jerez, and Santiago. At the end of the three years, they told us: ‘sorry, now full fares.’ And we said: ‘if the fees are the same as Barcelona, then we’ll put more flights to Barcelona.’ So we pulled one million seats from Spanish regional airports this winter, again, and said this is happening because they charge us the same rates.” Where are you reallocating those seats?“Half a million were shifted to Palma, Malaga, Madrid, and Barcelona, and the other half million went to regions in Italy. The Spanish central government and Aena blame Ryanair, because apparently we’re a band of pirates and bloody extortionists, or whatever.” But isn’t the point that you’ve become so powerful you’re calling the shots?“We always have. When we opened at Ciampino, we were leveraging Ciampino to get into Fiumicino. And we got into Fiumicino. We opened a base in Trapani but only after Sicily scrapped the municipal tax. We’re trying to leverage that to get it abolished elsewhere in Italy too.” Don’t you think you’re abusing that position?“Not at all. We’re campaigning for lower costs and lower taxes on air travel. My job is to shout, complain, protest against regional airports in Spain, against the municipal tax in Italy. Meanwhile, we’re growing like crazy in Poland. We’re growing in the UK. That’s what we have to do.” How are ticket prices trending?“Last year average fares fell 7%. This year we think they’ll almost fully recover. So we estimate a 6–7% rise this year, which means that over a two-year period, 2025 fares will be the same as in 2023. At a time when everything else is going up, we’re still charging the same bloody fares as two years ago. But we’re carrying 20% more passengers.” From November 12, boarding passes will only be available through your app. But there are concerns about people who may struggle with the procedures, like the elderly.“I’m elderly too. Every time we’ve made a tech change, someone has said the elderly won’t manage. But the first to adapt were the elderly: they have plenty of time and even if they’re not good with digital tools, they ask their children or grandchildren.” But what happens from November 12?“If some elderly person shows up with a bloody piece of paper, we’ll take it anyway. We’ll accept printed boarding passes throughout the winter.” Why this ‘full digital’ decision?“Look at what happened with the cyberattack on Collins Aerospace in Berlin and Brussels: if you’ve got everything on your phone, you get real-time updates.” Recently you increased to €2.5 the bonus for gate staff who catch people boarding with a trolley not included in the ticket or oversized baggage. Are you becoming a nasty airline?“No. One reason we’re raising it from €1 to €2.5 is that the number of bags at the gate is dropping fast. Now we have fewer than one bag at the gate every 10 flights. I personally check gates in Dublin and someone shows up with a backpack—usually an American—and says they didn’t know. Sorry, they still have to pay the penalty.” So you’re nasty.“No. We’re just enforcing our rules. Don’t think you’ll get away with it, because you won’t. But if everyone complied—if we managed to get everyone to travel with just a small bag or the small cabin bag—boarding would be faster. Everything becomes more efficient. And the reason we’re not a nasty airline is that we keep lowering fares.” lberberi@corriere.it

title: Wizz Air CEO: “Back to growth, we'll be the second airline in Italy and we are ready for a price war with Ryanair”
content:
Trump attacca la Ue: «Nazioni decadenti guidate da leader deboli». E paragona Zelensky all'imprenditore del circo P. T. Barnum Salva questo articolo e leggilo quando vuoi. Il servizio è dedicato agli utenti registrati. Trovi tutti gli articoli salvati nella tua area personale nella sezione preferiti e sull'app Corriere News. A chat with József Váradi. Wizz Air’s big pivot: from Abu Dhabi missteps to a renewed European focus. And the reply to Ryanair CEO words: "Pure garbage" In the years when air transport was posting record profits, Wizz Air — the Hungarian low-cost carrier that quickly became a key player in European skies — had to deal with several critical issues. Partly due to bad luck (the closure of the Ukrainian market after the Russian invasion or tensions in the Middle East), and partly due to strategic missteps (such as the decision not to hedge against fuel price fluctuations). As if that weren’t enough, the airline later had to ground dozens of next-generation aircraft because of engine problems that are dragging down the sector on a global scale. “We need to be patient until 2027, when our engine issues should be resolved,” Wizz Air CEO József Váradi explains in a long conversation with Corriere. He also announces a major comeback in Italy — where the airline will have to contend with Ryanair.    



 Wizz Air has had a couple of not-so-positive years…“2025 was a transitional year. We returned to growth after a flat 2024, partly caused by the grounding of aircraft due to Pratt & Whitney engine issues.” But the results still aren’t brilliant.“We are still affected by some of the ‘black swans’ we experienced earlier, including geopolitics. Even last summer we had to cancel all operations in Israel due to regional tensions.” How serious are the Pratt & Whitney engine problems?“We had 35 aircraft grounded. Next summer we expect 25 to be out of service. It will take another two years to get through the grounded-fleet phase. So we have this dichotomy: we’re growing again, which is positive, but we still have to manage inefficiencies linked to geopolitical issues and the engine problems.” József Váradi, Wizz Air CEO This year you paid the price for some not-so-successful past decisions: I’m referring to the purchase of the Airbus A321XLR — later scaled back — and the shutdown of operations in Abu Dhabi.“We made important decisions and we’re waiting to see the results. There’s a gap between when you decide and when the benefits actually materialize. 2025 was a crucial year not only to return to growth but also to adapt the business, address weaknesses and strengthen our advantages. We fixed the XLR program, shut down Abu Dhabi, but at the same time invested heavily in Central Europe: that’s where we come from, that’s where we’re well known and perform best.” So those were wrong decisions?“They were made in a very different context. The context changed, and that forced us to reconsider them. With hindsight, perhaps we would have preferred not to make them — but at the time it didn’t look that way. You learn from everything and adapt to the changing nature of the business.” For 2026 you’re focusing on three pillars: Central and Eastern Europe, Italy, and Israel. Let’s start with the first.“Central and Eastern Europe is a natural choice for us. GDPs continue to grow disproportionately compared to Western Europe, generating more disposable income and thus more traffic. We’re opening many new markets.” And Italy? Over the years you’ve reduced domestic operations.“In Italy we’re seeing significant improvement, even though it’s a rather saturated market. We are now focused on international traffic. But there are also domestic routes we like, though we must consider the development of other transport modes, such as trains, which limit certain opportunities.” How are your operations performing in Italy?“Very well. We invested heavily in the post-Covid phase and now we’re seeing brand maturation. I’m optimistic and enthusiastic: we can do much more. Sure, some airports are saturated — Catania and Naples come to mind — but even there, opportunities exist.” 
                    
                    
                di  Leonard Berberi 

 Aren’t you worried about Ryanair’s dominance?“No. It’s not Ryanair versus Wizz: that’s their narrative. The reality is that Wizz and Ryanair compete with everyone else. In Italy, perhaps ITA with Lufthansa will have more financial strength. But in 2026, with the expected growth, Wizz will be the country’s second-largest airline: a remarkable result.” So will there be a price war in Italy in 2026?“The market needs competition. We’ll be a good challenger. Where opportunities arise, we’ll take them. With or without Ryanair, we will continue to grow.” This will inevitably trigger a fare war with Ryanair.“Well, then let it be a war.” Several European markets are saturated. Where do you see room for growth?“Beyond Central Europe and Italy, London as well.” There is talk of opening a base in Tel Aviv, a complex market: Ryanair is pulling out, citing high operating costs. Yet you want to invest there.“I’d add a caveat. We are an airline that flies to Israel. We are talking with the government to understand whether operations can be improved and whether a base makes sense. But no decision has been made: it’s an ongoing discussion, nothing final.” What’s the issue? Incentives?“No. The Israeli government is very concerned about the cost of living. During the war, local carriers took advantage of the situation and raised prices. Now the government wants to bring in efficient airlines to create competition and lower travel costs, easing the cost of living.” You’re targeting a 500-aircraft fleet, yet you postponed delivery of 88 aircraft from 2030 to 2033. Why?“When we placed the order in 2017, we planned to grow 15% annually. Two years ago we lost a significant portion of the fleet due to the engine grounding, which we’ll recover only by the end of 2027. We had to adjust the delivery flow to avoid unsustainable growth spikes of 30–40%.” You also canceled most of your A321XLR order.“We converted the majority of XLRs into A321neos.” 
                    
                    
                di  Leonard Berberi 

 Many airlines are negotiating with Pratt & Whitney — some are considering legal action. Where do you stand?“We have an agreement through the end of 2027 for a compensation plan. It covers grounding costs, not lost opportunities. It’s a moderate plan compared to total exposure, but it was the best deal possible.” O’Leary accuses you of running a “Ponzi scheme” with sale & leaseback. Now it seems you're abandoning that practice.“We haven’t abandoned anything. We continue to do sale & leaseback. We’re simply rebalancing: today 80% of the fleet is on operating lease and 20% in some form of ownership.” How do you respond to O’Leary’s accusation?“What he says is pure garbage. It’s simply wrong.” Why?“Take an aircraft that costs 100 dollars. If you do sale & leaseback, you sell it for 110, cashing in 10. Then you amortize the 110 over 10 years, at a cost of 11 per year. If you own it, the 100 dollars are amortized over 10 years, 10 per year. You can’t ignore the 10 million you cash in by only looking at costs. And only one-third of those 10 million is profit in the first year — the rest spreads over the lease period. It’s a different financial approach. Sale & leaseback gives you cash upfront, not profit. Ownership gives more profit and less cash.” O’Leary also says you abandon markets when Ryanair enters.“That’s a lie. Look at the numbers: in Budapest we’ve been competing for fifteen years and we are 25–30% larger. In Romania we are three times bigger. In Warsaw, three times bigger. In Bratislava, bigger as well. I can’t believe he’s that poorly informed — he’s lying.” He argues that only a few European carriers will survive: IAG, Lufthansa Group, Air France–KLM and Ryanair. Wizz and easyJet will disappear.“We’re not going anywhere. Our liquidity is higher than theirs. The issue is not cash but turning it into profits, hindered by external factors. Once the engine issue is resolved, profitability will rise again.” 
                    
                    
                di  Leonard Berberi 

 O’Leary also claims you’ll be part of consolidation.“He’s always said that, and he has always been wrong. Why would he be right now? We have no plan to be acquired. We just received our 250th aircraft. We carry more passengers as a brand than Lufthansa, Air France and British Airways. Ryanair is bigger, sure, but it started 25 years earlier. If you compare Ryanair at our age, it wasn’t even close to our size. We have done very well.” Would Wizz ever be an active player in M&A?“Low-cost carriers avoid complexities because they create costs that destroy the model. And mergers are complex: fleet, systems, people. We will grow organically by 10 million passengers a year. Why complicate things?” At one point you were interested in easyJet…“We simply had a look. We never signed an NDA, never even launched a process.” Your discounted-ticket subscription ‘All You Can Fly’ continues. Ryanair shut down ‘Prime’ after eight months. Why?“We serve different segments. Twenty years ago we transported Eastern European workers to the West. Today we transport tourists, people visiting family, business travelers. Each has different needs. Some are interested in All You Can Fly, others in low fares at the time of purchase.” So you’ll continue with segmentation?“I think so.” You introduced, experimentally, a free middle seat in the first rows for business passengers. Will you expand it?“We’ll see. That seat is often already empty because we charge a premium for the first row. Internally the seat is often empty: we’re just monetizing it differently.” Your plans once included intercontinental flights via Abu Dhabi using XLRs. Is that project still viable from other bases?“We have to be realistic. The engine issue is significant. I can’t increase exposure in challenging climates like the Middle East or India. This is not the time. We converted most XLRs into A321neos. If in five years the technology improves, we’ll go back to Airbus.” lberberi@corriere.it

title: Expo Tree Plagiarism Charge
content:
It has been hailed as Italy’s Eiffel Tower. And that’s what it may well turn out to be. However, one thing is certain. No one ever called the Eiffel Tower a rip-off. And yesterday, the Albero della vita [Tree of Life] was called just that. After months of squabbling, the future emblem of Expo 2015 as a whole and Padiglione Italia - the Italian pavilion - in particular, received the final green light three days ago from corruption tsar Raffaele Cantone. But there was barely time to think about starting work before Chris Wilkinson happened to see a photo of the project. The British architect’s first thought was that the Albero looked just like his Singapore Supertrees. He chuckled and phoned his lawyers. 
Gardens by the Bay The British architect recognised the resemblance for the simple reason that they were designed by his friend and colleague Andrew Grant, who included them in the 54 spectacular hectares of Singapore’s Gardens by the Bay designed by Wilkinson himself in 2006. The multiple award-winning project is well-known to architects all over the world. When we spoke to him over the phone at the practice where he coordinates 150 collaborators, Wilkinson again declared himself “simply surprised” and unable to imagine that the whoever designed the Albero was unaware of the Gardens. The idea is too similar to the Supertrees to be a coincidence. Photographs back him up but there are some differences. Singapore has eighteen trees linked by an aerial walkway, the tallest are fifty metres high and feature a panoramic bistro. Grant’s Supertrees are clad in 160,000 plants from 200 different species, their branches were designed by algorithm and their Disneyland-style special effects are solar-powered. 


 
RightsThe Albero della vita is just a drawing for the moment but it will stand about thirty-seven metres tall and be made of wood and cement. A competition will be notified “this week” for the illuminations technology, as the Expo commissioner Giuseppe Sala pointed out yesterday. The competition will take two months and then the Albero will have to be built and handed over, it is hoped by the end of May. Yesterday Vincente Loscertales, secretary of the Bureau International des Expositions, was in Milan. His comment was: “All right then. It just means it’ll be a Bonsai of Life”. The man who dreamed up the Albero is Marco Balich. He is the major events organiser appointed at the recommendation of Italian pavilion commissioner, Diana Bracco. Balich received compensation of €2 million in the form of indirect consultancy but declined any royalties deriving from future exploitation of his Tree of Life. Others are counting on those royalties: the sponsor Pirelli, the Coldiretti farmers’ association and the Orgoglio Brescia pool of companies that will be tasked with building at least the wood and cement part, and will have to share the budget of €8 million with the other two partners. But one or two chickens may have been counted unhatched. Wilkinson did not mention the word royalty yet his position on the issue is crystal clear: “No decision has been taken. But we as a practice are consulting our lawyers, of course, to find the most appropriate way to protect the ownership of an idea we believe is ours, admired by ten million visitors in the last two years alone”. His point of view - art critic Vittorio Sgarbi had already advanced the suspicion - is shared by architects in Italy such as Milan-based Alessandro Zoppini, a man who knows about major projects having worked on, among others, the Turin and Sochi Olympics: “Technically, the Albero della vita is an obvious reworking of the Singapore project. Nowadays, we Italians copy the Asians”. Sad to think that when the project received the go-ahead the other day, the president of Coldiretti Lombardia, Ettore Prandini, proudly announced: “Expo will be an opportunity to defend Italian-made products against fakes all over the world”. Egg-on-face time, perhaps. 
 English translation by Giles Watson www.watson.it Article in Italian I PIÚ VISTI

title: Ita Airways narrows losses despite engine woes and grounded jets: CEO Eberhart eyes break-even by end 2025
content:
Trump attacca la Ue: «Nazioni decadenti guidate da leader deboli». E paragona Zelensky all'imprenditore del circo P. T. Barnum Salva questo articolo e leggilo quando vuoi. Il servizio è dedicato agli utenti registrati. Trovi tutti gli articoli salvati nella tua area personale nella sezione preferiti e sull'app Corriere News. In the first six months: €1.5 billion in revenue and a negative EBIT of €46 million, improved by almost 50%. The airline’s CEO: satisfied with domestic flights and South America Joerg Eberhart, Ita Airways’ CEO and DG (pic by LaPresse) In the first six months of this year, ITA Airways improved its operating result by almost 50%. The bottom line, however, remains in the red, net of the positive — and extraordinary — effect of the favorable euro-dollar exchange rate. “But the airline is on the right track to break even as early as 2025,” says CEO and general manager Joerg Eberhart in a conversation with Corriere. Eberhart, who has been at the helm since last January — when Lufthansa acquired a 41% stake in ITA — does not hide the challenges, even though the summer quarter is closing with a profit. At the moment, about a fifth of the airline’s fleet — around twenty aircraft — are grounded for various reasons, according to data from the specialized platform ch-aviation. This has had a negative impact on revenues, the manager confirms. Meanwhile, unions are stirring, demanding — among other things — a more ambitious 2026–2030 business plan than the one announced in July. But Eberhart’s mantra, repeated several times, is simple: no money is wasted (and it is also public money — the other 59% is owned by the Ministry of Economy) on aggressive, risky expansion; no routes are launched that don’t make sense on paper, let alone in reality. The goal is to put the company in a position to start generating profits.    



 Let’s start with the data from the first half of this year. “We carried 8.1 million passengers — in line with last year, but with a 15% increase in intercontinental flights. The average load factor was 82%, compared to 79% in 2024. On the financial side, total revenues were €1.5 billion, €100 million more than last year.” And at the bottom line of the financial statement? “In the first six months we achieved an EBIT of -€46 million, an improvement of €40 million compared to the same period in 2024.” What about net results? “We are doing better than last year, even ahead of budget.” Still negative, then. “We have a negative EBIT of €46 million, then there are leasing contracts worth about €50 million. So we are still about €100 million in the red.” But the months when money is made are still to come in the accounts. “Yes. July, August, September, and October are all strong months. In November and December, we lose more or less what we earn in October, so the result at the end of September often coincides with the final result.” So, how did the summer go? “Between July and August we carried almost 3 million passengers, 14% fewer than in the same two-month period last year, with a load factor of 86% (compared to 84% in 2024). Revenues fell 6%, less than passengers: this means the margin was better.” Why this drop in passengers while the market grew? “The main cause is problems with Pratt & Whitney’s new-generation engines, which affect the entire industry. They are installed on our Airbus A320neos and A220s. These problems are more serious than we expected six months ago: we had estimated 8 grounded planes this year, but instead we have 15.” Almost double. How is that possible? “Because when you open an engine for inspection, you find problems that you can’t see from the outside, so you have to intervene.” But there are another 5 planes grounded… “This is due to maintenance, which challenges us to optimize our interaction with Atitech, the company that handles it. Then there are three other planes leased to easyJet as part of the package of sacrifices imposed by the EU in exchange for authorizing Lufthansa’s investment.” You were diplomatic, but there are some issues with maintenance: there are jets being delivered later than expected, forcing you to cancel flights at the last minute. “That’s right. But sometimes this also happens because a part is missing, given the global shortage of spare parts that has been ongoing for some time.” Some argue that the agreement with Atitech is unfavorable to ITA. “My predecessors signed the contract when ITA was in a very particular situation: they had to find a maintenance solution very quickly, and Atitech was the only one to step forward. The current agreement has weaknesses that we will have to work through together.” When does the contract with Atitech expire?“In 2031, with the possibility of an initial review this November: we can compare Atitech’s conditions — both economic and quality-wise — with those of the rest of the sector and see where we can improve together.” So ITA officially has a fleet of 100 aircraft, but can actually use 77. “And in the meantime, we are still paying the leasing installments for the grounded jets.” Will you seek compensation from the engine manufacturer? “We already have.” How much are the damages? “I can’t reveal the figure, there is a non-disclosure agreement. But I can say they are significant. Also because there are additional impacts: lost passengers, even on intercontinental flights, because of the reduction in short- and medium-haul connections that ‘feed’ the long-haul routes. And then we have an excess of A220 pilots who are currently flying very little: it’s unacceptable that they work only 20–25 hours per month because of the engines.” So a 2025 more complicated than expected. “Definitely. And I agree that it’s not normal for a company with 100 aircraft to end up flying only 77.” When do you think you’ll get out of this situation? “I hope to see an improvement in 2027. 2026 will still be a difficult year, though with more stable planning.” How many planes do you expect to have grounded next year? “Ten to fifteen. But mitigated by extending some leasing contracts, so as to reduce the impact to 5–7 aircraft.” But is there still a chance of reaching operating break-even this year? “Yes. The second half of the year is better than the first because of the summer peak, so we aim to get very close to break-even. Low fuel prices are helping us, and we have also done some internal cost management: the positive effect compared to the budget is thanks to these two factors.” How have ITA’s different markets performed? “Very well in the domestic segment (flights within Italy), Canada, and South America. We are very pleased. Japan is also doing well, as are Switzerland, France, Tunisia, and Algeria.” And the United States, your main source of profit? “Results are basically in line with last year, so that’s positive. Dakar and Accra are performing better than expected. We are also seeing good results from Bangkok and — on seasonal routes — from the Spanish and Greek islands.” What are you not happy with? “For now, India, perhaps also due to difficulties in issuing visas. Then Germany, Belgium, and the Netherlands — in this case as a direct consequence of the sacrifices imposed at Milan Linate by the EU antitrust authority. Many rivals have moved flights to the city airport using the slots we were forced to give up. Then there is Egypt, which is a bit weak, partly due to the crisis in the Middle East.” You have not yet resumed flights to Tel Aviv, which was a profitable destination for you. What impact does this have on your accounts?“Looking at the May–October period this year, we estimate €50 million less in revenue.” When will new routes to South America be launched?“At first — and I mean over the next one or two years — it’s more likely that we will increase capacity on existing routes: Buenos Aires, Rio de Janeiro, and São Paulo.” And elsewhere? “We have studied other destinations in the region at length.” There is talk of Bogotá, in Colombia. “But there the problem is that the airport is at high altitude, which would force our Airbus A350s and A330neos to fly with about a hundred fewer passengers on return flights to Italy: economically, it is very difficult to achieve a positive result.” There are also rumors of Lima and Santiago de Chile. “We have analyzed those as well. They are long routes that only fill up at low fares. When the fare goes up, nobody books anymore. That said, we will evaluate year by year, perhaps also taking into account possible incentives.” And what about North America? “It will depend a lot on joining the A++ transatlantic joint venture with United Airlines and Air Canada planned for 2027 (an agreement that allows partners to coordinate routes, schedules, prices, and offers). Once we are in, we will be able to move into hubs where United is strong, such as Newark and Houston: those will also become the nodes where ITA meets its partner.” The unions are restless and threatening a strike. They are calling for a bolder 2026–2030 business plan, with greater expansion than you have planned.“I understand their reasoning and that they want to renegotiate the contract, which expired last year. But we must reach break-even. The sooner we do, the sooner we can decide on more aggressive growth. I would stress that the effects of synergies with the Lufthansa group and its partners will be seen mostly in 2027. And then we must take the markets into account.” In what sense?“We must grow because there is a market that justifies it. I’m happy if more jobs are created, but this must happen without operating at a loss.” The strike threat is a bit of a sign that the honeymoon is over. “We are in constant dialogue with them. ITA needed a strong partner: Lufthansa is already a guarantee of this company’s stability. It creates prospects and ensures solidity.” Don’t you think the business plan could have been bolder, as employee representatives claim? “They argue that the market is expanding, especially in Rome, and therefore ITA should grow more to avoid losing market share. I don’t entirely disagree. But our task is also to expand without going into the red.” As early as June 2026 Lufthansa could exercise its option to raise its stake to 90%. “I think this is a tangible sign of Lufthansa’s commitment: they believe in ITA. That can only be positive.” You could bring more passengers into the Rome hub through feeder agreements with regional carriers. “We are looking around, perhaps with airlines already working with Lufthansa, such as AirBaltic or Braathens.” And what about Air Dolomiti, which is part of the Lufthansa Group? “Air Dolomiti is based in Verona, flies frequently to Venice, and has an operating base in Florence — all locations that are interesting for us at ITA to connect with Fiumicino. We could plan something with them, perhaps not in 2026.” lberberi@corriere.it

They are installed on our Airbus A320neos and A220s. The current agreement has weaknesses that we will have to work through together.” When does the contract with Atitech expire?“In 2031, with the possibility of an initial review this November: we can compare Atitech’s conditions — both economic and quality-wise — with those of the rest of the sector and see where we can improve together.” So ITA officially has a fleet of 100 aircraft, but can actually use 77. “And in the meantime, we are still paying the leasing installments for the grounded jets.” Will you seek compensation from the engine manufacturer? “We already have.” How much are the damages? “I can’t reveal the figure, there is a non-disclosure agreement. title: New routes and more efficiency: Eberhart eyes sustainable profit for Ita Airways amid legacy and reform
content:
Trump attacca la Ue: «Nazioni decadenti guidate da leader deboli». E paragona Zelensky all'imprenditore del circo P. T. Barnum Salva questo articolo e leggilo quando vuoi. Il servizio è dedicato agli utenti registrati. Trovi tutti gli articoli salvati nella tua area personale nella sezione preferiti e sull'app Corriere News. The first interview with the CEO of the Italian flag carrier co-owned by the Ministry of Economy and Lufthansa. Work is underway on the new industrial plan. “First profit expected in 2027.” Joerg Eberhart, CEO and general manager of Ita Airways (pic LaPresse) “There’s quite a bit of work to do, but the company’s fundamentals are solid, and with everyone’s effort we can generate profits and grow further.” Joerg Eberhart, the Italo-German CEO and General Manager of ITA Airways since January 17, has no doubts: the airline — 59% owned by the Ministry of Economy and 41% by Lufthansa — can achieve what its predecessor, Alitalia, never managed in decades: closing its financial statements in the black. But for that to happen, the machine’s gears must run smoothly. In 2024, under full MEF (Ministry of Economy and Finance) management, ITA reported €3.1 billion in revenues, a positive EBIT of €3 million, and a net loss of €227 million. “But profit is possible,” says Eberhart in his first interview as CEO, given from his office at the airline’s headquarters near Fiumicino Airport. How did the first quarter go under MEF-Lufthansa management?“Total revenue from January to March was €637 million, of which €558 million came from passenger revenues, including extras like seat selection, checked baggage, and class upgrades.”    



 And the EBIT?“It’s negative. We don’t have a final figure yet because some cost items are still to be included.” You previously worked as a manager at Lufthansa and followed the Italian airline from the German side. How many times did you try to invest?“Three times. The first was with Alitalia, managed by commissioners Luigi Gubitosi, Stefano Paleari, and Enrico Laghi. But we couldn’t reach an agreement.” And the second attempt?“Again with Alitalia, we worked on a possible consortium with Ferrovie dello Stato and Atlantia. But again, we couldn’t agree on a common business plan.” Then Alitalia shuts down and ITA takes off.“That’s when we stepped forward with MSC.” Experts considered the offer with the maritime giant to be the best.“I thought so too. But it didn’t turn out that way. The then-leaders at MEF chose the other proposal (from the Certares fund).” Disappointed?“Yes. We did everything we could. But that’s when Carsten (Spohr, Lufthansa Group CEO) told me: ‘You’ll see, our time will come because we are ITA’s natural partner.’” Then in autumn 2022 — new government, new economy minister — someone from Rome contacts you after the failure of the negotiations with Certares.“We get a call from MEF. Spohr and I go to Rome, and Minister Giancarlo Giorgetti asks if we’re still interested. And so we reopen the file.” And after a long process, the EU gives the green light, the merger happens, and a new board is installed with you as CEO and Sandro Pappalardo as chairman. Did government stability help?“It was crucial. We didn’t have to start from scratch. We dealt with four administrations: Conte I, Conte II, Draghi, and Meloni. All were involved to some degree, but the continuity of the current government was very important. Otherwise, I don’t know how it would’ve turned out.” Still, there were some frictions with MEF.“That’s normal. We were dealing with a government selling a company, not a private deal. The government has different needs. But we always found solutions, thanks to Antonino Turicchi (former ITA chairman), who built many bridges between MEF and Lufthansa.” Was there a moment when you thought “it’s all going to fall apart”?“There were several. One time in spring 2024, after yet another exchange with the EU, we got a call from Brussels saying that if things didn’t change, the deal would be stopped.” Another moment?“Last November, when we didn’t send the remedies package to the Commission due to disagreements on some points. I went home in the middle of the night thinking: it’s over.” Did you have a Plan B?“Yes. If the EU had rejected the deal, we had considered a 20% investment instead of 41%, like IAG did with Air Europa or Air France-KLM with SAS.” When did you realize you had succeeded?“When the Commission gave the go-ahead. Though I didn’t expect it to remain difficult, especially with the requirement to find so-called ‘remedy takers’.” Before ITA, you were Head of Strategy at Lufthansa. Why invest in an Italian airline? What value do you see?“The Italian market is highly valuable, a mix of business and inbound tourism, strong purchasing power, and Rome Fiumicino has a geographical advantage toward South America and Africa. The hub works very well — unlike some other Lufthansa group hubs where growth is no longer possible. Frankfurt and Munich have also had various operational issues in the past three years. So, Italy was the ideal opportunity for us. We’ve also had experience with Air Dolomiti and Lufthansa Technik.” What kind of company did you find when you arrived?“A company with a very professional and motivated team that knows what it’s doing. Though some processes weren’t as structured as in a mature company. There’s definitely potential to be developed.” ITA’s route network looks a lot like Alitalia’s. What will the next industrial plan be based on?“We’re working on it with MEF and will also discuss it with unions. On the network level, we’re looking at a mix of new markets and strengthening existing routes.” How many new routes?“We’ve identified five interesting intercontinental connections and will evaluate those.” To where?“We already have enough in South America. We’re missing some North American routes. Then it depends on the Russia situation — if we can fly over it again, some Asian destinations become interesting.” Further afield? Like Australia?“We don’t have aircraft capable of such long routes. Plus, that market has been taken over by Middle Eastern carriers — now everything goes through Istanbul, Dubai, Doha, Abu Dhabi. In the future, Saudi and Indian airlines will also play a role.” Has Europe lost control of traffic to the Pacific?“I’m afraid so.” Once fully operational, how much will Lufthansa and the transatlantic joint venture with United Airlines and Air Canada contribute to ITA’s revenues?“Much of it isn’t measurable. Maybe only a third of the expected revenue is truly quantifiable. Still, it should contribute over €100 million a year.” As part of the EU’s required “remedies” for the merger, you had to give up many slots at Milan Linate. What did that cost you?“Not all the slots we held were actually profitable.” So they did you a favor?“Not quite. The airlines that got those slots are now competing directly on some routes.” For example?“KLM, for instance. They got some slots and moved their Amsterdam flights from Malpensa to Linate. We also fly that route, so we lost the benefit.” Is ITA abandoning Malpensa for good?“Alitalia’s intercontinental flights from Malpensa never worked well. Today, the competition is fierce — even Emirates flies to New York. Starting long-haul flights from Malpensa would require a supporting network of short and medium-haul routes, which isn’t feasible for an airline like ours with only about 100 planes.” So better to focus on one hub?“We must concentrate on Rome. We need to build critical mass at Fiumicino, increase sales, and expand long-haul destinations instead of investing in a new hub.” 
                    
                    
                di  Leonard Berberi 

 What should ITA’s positioning be?“We must reach higher and higher levels of customer satisfaction in every part of the travel experience.” How far along are you?“There’s positive progress, but we’re not yet at the level of the best Lufthansa group carriers. We need to improve operations and onboard connectivity.” U.S. airlines offer free instant messaging, for instance.“More and more carriers are doing that. If it becomes a trend, we’ll need to act on it too.” You’ve been a paratrooper, flight attendant, Airbus A320 captain (you still are), and a manager. What’s helped you most at ITA?“Operational experience, definitely. Also the four years I spent as an entrepreneur. Coming here felt like joining a startup.” Airlines increasingly focus on cost management.“Cost discipline is essential — the foundation — but we also need more focus on how production factors are combined.” Meaning?“Look at aircraft productivity: how many hours they fly per day. Then there’s the issue of labor contracts. These are all operational cost factors that must be constantly monitored.” Are you satisfied with aircraft productivity?“There’s room for improvement. Long-haul aircraft are productive. Less so for short and medium-haul ones. Engine issues are a factor, as well as the limited number of ‘waves’ at our Fiumicino hub.” By ‘waves’ you mean peak times when short-haul flights bring passengers to the hub to connect to long-haul routes.“We have three: one in the morning and two smaller ones during the day, though few long-haul flights depart in those.” How can that change?“With more flights to the East or South Africa, we could strengthen the second and third waves. We need to grow the long-haul fleet to create stronger waves throughout the day. With stronger waves, the short-haul feeder network works better. That increases productivity.” But would you still stick to three waves?“For now, yes.” 
                    
                    
                di  Leonard Berberi 

 In autumn, you’ll begin talks with unions on contract renewals. You’ve said this is a year of consolidation — a way of saying “don’t expect too much.”“We need to reach sustainable breakeven. It must be true breakeven, not just due to low fuel prices. I’m referring to ‘CASK ex-fuel,’ or the unit cost excluding fuel.” So company growth means more jobs?“Yes — more jobs and career growth. A first officer should have the chance to become captain after a number of years. That only happens with growth.” Clearly, the unions will want pay raises.“It’s right that they have proposals. We’ll see what’s feasible. There are areas where we can create mutual benefits to share with employees.” What EBIT are you aiming for this year?“I’d like to reach at least +€50 million. That would be a good result. But it depends a lot on what happens in the second half of the year.” When will the real profit come?“If all goes as planned, a sustainable profit should be possible in 2027. But I want improvement in other areas too — like our Net Promoter Score (customer satisfaction) and our digital innovation index, which are strategic indicators.” What challenges do you foresee in 2025?“European air traffic control remains an issue. So do airports. Fiumicino has grown faster than expected, and Aeroporti di Roma (Fiumicino’s operator) needs to bring investments forward.” So is a fourth runway at Fiumicino urgent?“Experts say it’s necessary. We’re talking with AdR’s management about how ITA can contribute to the projected growth.” Italian politics has always interfered with Alitalia and ITA’s day-to-day affairs. Have they started interfering with you too?(laughs) “No, no. After all, this is an important investment.” lberberi@corriere.it

text_combined:
New routes and more efficiency: Eberhart eyes sustainable profit for Ita Airways amid legacy and reform Trump attacca la Ue: «Nazioni decadenti guidate da leader deboli». E paragona Zelensky all'imprenditore del circo P. T. Barnum Salva questo articolo e leggilo quando vuoi. Il servizio è dedicato agli utenti registrati. Trovi tutti gli articoli salvati nella tua area personale nella sezione preferiti e sull'app Corriere News. The first interview with the CEO of the Italian flag carrier co-owned by the Ministry of Economy and Lufthansa. Work is underway on the new industrial plan. “First profit expected in 2027.” Joerg Eberhart, CEO and general manager of Ita Airways (pic LaPresse) “There’s quite a bit of work to do, but the company’s fundamentals are solid, and with everyone’s effort we can generate profits and grow further.” Joerg Eberhart, the Italo-German CEO and General Manager of ITA Airways since January 17, has no doubts: the airline — 59% owned by the Ministry of Economy and 41% by Lufthansa — can achieve what its predecessor, Alitalia, never managed in decades: closing its financial statements in the black. But for that to happen, the machine’s gears must run smoothly. In 2024, under full MEF (Ministry of Economy and Finance) management, ITA reported €3.1 billion in revenues, a positive EBIT of €3 million, and a net loss of €227 million. “But profit is possible,” says Eberhart in his first interview as CEO, given from his office at the airline’s headquarters near Fiumicino Airport. How did the first quarter go under MEF-Lufthansa management?“Total revenue from January to March was €637 million, of which €558 million came from passenger revenues, including extras like seat selection, checked baggage, and class upgrades.”    



 And the EBIT?“It’s negative. We don’t have a final figure yet because some cost items are still to be included.” You previously worked as a manager at Lufthansa and followed the Italian airline from the German side. How many times did you try to invest?“Three times. The first was with Alitalia, managed by commissioners Luigi Gubitosi, Stefano Paleari, and Enrico Laghi. But we couldn’t reach an agreement.” And the second attempt?“Again with Alitalia, we worked on a possible consortium with Ferrovie dello Stato and Atlantia. But again, we couldn’t agree on a common business plan.” Then Alitalia shuts down and ITA takes off.“That’s when we stepped forward with MSC.” Experts considered the offer with the maritime giant to be the best.“I thought so too. But it didn’t turn out that way. The then-leaders at MEF chose the other proposal (from the Certares fund).” Disappointed?“Yes. We did everything we could. But that’s when Carsten (Spohr, Lufthansa Group CEO) told me: ‘You’ll see, our time will come because we are ITA’s natural partner.’” Then in autumn 2022 — new government, new economy minister — someone from Rome contacts you after the failure of the negotiations with Certares.“We get a call from MEF. Spohr and I go to Rome, and Minister Giancarlo Giorgetti asks if we’re still interested. And so we reopen the file.” And after a long process, the EU gives the green light, the merger happens, and a new board is installed with you as CEO and Sandro Pappalardo as chairman. Did government stability help?“It was crucial. We didn’t have to start from scratch. We dealt with four administrations: Conte I, Conte II, Draghi, and Meloni. All were involved to some degree, but the continuity of the current government was very important. Otherwise, I don’t know how it would’ve turned out.” Still, there were some frictions with MEF.“That’s normal. We were dealing with a government selling a company, not a private deal. The government has different needs. But we always found solutions, thanks to Antonino Turicchi (former ITA chairman), who built many bridges between MEF and Lufthansa.” Was there a moment when you thought “it’s all going to fall apart”?“There were several. One time in spring 2024, after yet another exchange with the EU, we got a call from Brussels saying that if things didn’t change, the deal would be stopped.” Another moment?“Last November, when we didn’t send the remedies package to the Commission due to disagreements on some points. I went home in the middle of the night thinking: it’s over.” Did you have a Plan B?“Yes. If the EU had rejected the deal, we had considered a 20% investment instead of 41%, like IAG did with Air Europa or Air France-KLM with SAS.” When did you realize you had succeeded?“When the Commission gave the go-ahead. Though I didn’t expect it to remain difficult, especially with the requirement to find so-called ‘remedy takers’.” Before ITA, you were Head of Strategy at Lufthansa. Why invest in an Italian airline? What value do you see?“The Italian market is highly valuable, a mix of business and inbound tourism, strong purchasing power, and Rome Fiumicino has a geographical advantage toward South America and Africa. The hub works very well — unlike some other Lufthansa group hubs where growth is no longer possible. Frankfurt and Munich have also had various operational issues in the past three years. So, Italy was the ideal opportunity for us. We’ve also had experience with Air Dolomiti and Lufthansa Technik.” What kind of company did you find when you arrived?“A company with a very professional and motivated team that knows what it’s doing. Though some processes weren’t as structured as in a mature company. There’s definitely potential to be developed.” ITA’s route network looks a lot like Alitalia’s. What will the next industrial plan be based on?“We’re working on it with MEF and will also discuss it with unions. On the network level, we’re looking at a mix of new markets and strengthening existing routes.” How many new routes?“We’ve identified five interesting intercontinental connections and will evaluate those.” To where?“We already have enough in South America. We’re missing some North American routes. Then it depends on the Russia situation — if we can fly over it again, some Asian destinations become interesting.” Further afield? Like Australia?“We don’t have aircraft capable of such long routes. Plus, that market has been taken over by Middle Eastern carriers — now everything goes through Istanbul, Dubai, Doha, Abu Dhabi. In the future, Saudi and Indian airlines will also play a role.” Has Europe lost control of traffic to the Pacific?“I’m afraid so.” Once fully operational, how much will Lufthansa and the transatlantic joint venture with United Airlines and Air Canada contribute to ITA’s revenues?“Much of it isn’t measurable. Maybe only a third of the expected revenue is truly quantifiable. Still, it should contribute over €100 million a year.” As part of the EU’s required “remedies” for the merger, you had to give up many slots at Milan Linate. What did that cost you?“Not all the slots we held were actually profitable.” So they did you a favor?“Not quite. The airlines that got those slots are now competing directly on some routes.” For example?“KLM, for instance. They got some slots and moved their Amsterdam flights from Malpensa to Linate. We also fly that route, so we lost the benefit.” Is ITA abandoning Malpensa for good?“Alitalia’s intercontinental flights from Malpensa never worked well. Today, the competition is fierce — even Emirates flies to New York. Starting long-haul flights from Malpensa would require a supporting network of short and medium-haul routes, which isn’t feasible for an airline like ours with only about 100 planes.” So better to focus on one hub?“We must concentrate on Rome. We need to build critical mass at Fiumicino, increase sales, and expand long-haul destinations instead of investing in a new hub.” 
                    
                    
                di  Leonard Berberi 

 What should ITA’s positioning be?“We must reach higher and higher levels of customer satisfaction in every part of the travel experience.” How far along are you?“There’s positive progress, but we’re not yet at the level of the best Lufthansa group carriers. We need to improve operations and onboard connectivity.” U.S. airlines offer free instant messaging, for instance.“More and more carriers are doing that. If it becomes a trend, we’ll need to act on it too.” You’ve been a paratrooper, flight attendant, Airbus A320 captain (you still are), and a manager. What’s helped you most at ITA?“Operational experience, definitely. Also the four years I spent as an entrepreneur. Coming here felt like joining a startup.” Airlines increasingly focus on cost management.“Cost discipline is essential — the foundation — but we also need more focus on how production factors are combined.” Meaning?“Look at aircraft productivity: how many hours they fly per day. Then there’s the issue of labor contracts. These are all operational cost factors that must be constantly monitored.” Are you satisfied with aircraft productivity?“There’s room for improvement. Long-haul aircraft are productive. Less so for short and medium-haul ones. Engine issues are a factor, as well as the limited number of ‘waves’ at our Fiumicino hub.” By ‘waves’ you mean peak times when short-haul flights bring passengers to the hub to connect to long-haul routes.“We have three: one in the morning and two smaller ones during the day, though few long-haul flights depart in those.” How can that change?“With more flights to the East or South Africa, we could strengthen the second and third waves. We need to grow the long-haul fleet to create stronger waves throughout the day. With stronger waves, the short-haul feeder network works better. That increases productivity.” But would you still stick to three waves?“For now, yes.” 
                    
                    
                di  Leonard Berberi 

 In autumn, you’ll begin talks with unions on contract renewals. You’ve said this is a year of consolidation — a way of saying “don’t expect too much.”“We need to reach sustainable breakeven. It must be true breakeven, not just due to low fuel prices. I’m referring to ‘CASK ex-fuel,’ or the unit cost excluding fuel.” So company growth means more jobs?“Yes — more jobs and career growth. A first officer should have the chance to become captain after a number of years. That only happens with growth.” Clearly, the unions will want pay raises.“It’s right that they have proposals. We’ll see what’s feasible. There are areas where we can create mutual benefits to share with employees.” What EBIT are you aiming for this year?“I’d like to reach at least +€50 million. That would be a good result. But it depends a lot on what happens in the second half of the year.” When will the real profit come?“If all goes as planned, a sustainable profit should be possible in 2027. But I want improvement in other areas too — like our Net Promoter Score (customer satisfaction) and our digital innovation index, which are strategic indicators.” What challenges do you foresee in 2025?“European air traffic control remains an issue. So do airports. Fiumicino has grown faster than expected, and Aeroporti di Roma (Fiumicino’s operator) needs to bring investments forward.” So is a fourth runway at Fiumicino urgent?“Experts say it’s necessary. We’re talking with AdR’s management about how ITA can contribute to the projected growth.” Italian politics has always interfered with Alitalia and ITA’s day-to-day affairs. Have they started interfering with you too?(laughs) “No, no. After all, this is an important investment.” lberberi@corriere.it

title: Ita Airways plans over a dozen new long-haul routes to boost profitability
content:
Trump attacca la Ue: «Nazioni decadenti guidate da leader deboli». E paragona Zelensky all'imprenditore del circo P. T. Barnum Salva questo articolo e leggilo quando vuoi. Il servizio è dedicato agli utenti registrati. Trovi tutti gli articoli salvati nella tua area personale nella sezione preferiti e sull'app Corriere News. Work in progress on the 2026–2030 business plan of the Mef–Lufthansa-led carrier. New flights to Newark, Houston, Osaka, Singapore and Mumbai. Work underway on Africa and increased frequencies to South America An Ita Airways Airbus A350 at New York’s JFK Airport (photo by Vincenzo Pace) Ita Airways is considering launching over a dozen new intercontinental routes over the next five years from Rome Fiumicino to the US, Asia, and Africa, with the aim of boosting revenue and achieving sustainable profitability, according to people with knowledge of the discussions who emphasize the preliminary nature of the plans. Some routes will be "obvious choices," dictated by the presence of a partner at the destination who can ensure onward connections. Others will be chosen with the goal of improving the airline’s profit margins. In the background, the potential reopening of Russian airspace could bring the China dossier back into consideration. Ita declined to comment. Asked by Corriere della Sera on the sidelines of the IATA meetings in New Delhi, Ita’s CEO and general manager, Joerg Eberhart, refrained from making statements, stressing that the document is still in an early draft stage. At the airline’s headquarters — where since January Lufthansa (with a 41% stake) has joined the Italian Ministry of Economy and Finance (holding 59%) — work is underway on the development of the new 2026–2030 industrial plan, which remains highly confidential. One thing, however, is clear: the company needs to generate profits in order to have greater development opportunities within the Lufthansa Group.    



 
                    
                    
                di  Leonard Berberi 

 During a meeting with trade unions, CEO Joerg Eberhart revealed that the airline wants to focus more on long-haul operations, as these generate higher revenues compared to the domestic and European markets, where competition is much fiercer—on one side from low-cost carriers, and on the other from legacy airlines channeling traffic through their hubs. Currently, the plan is to increase the intercontinental fleet by one aircraft per year, totaling five aircraft: four Airbus A350s and one A330neo. Which routes are being considered? In South America—where Ita is seeing strong load factors and revenues—there are plans to increase flight frequencies, particularly to Buenos Aires. According to sources, this may also involve strengthening the agreement with Aerolíneas Argentinas. Eberhart was seen in intense discussions with the Argentine carrier’s leadership during the IATA meetings. In 2026, with the arrival of another Airbus A330neo, the Italian airline could launch the Rome Fiumicino–Mumbai (India) route, which would complement the existing service to New Delhi and benefit from the agreement with Air India. 2027 is expected to mark further expansion in the US, with direct flights to Newark—one of the New York’s airport—and Houston. Both are hubs for United Airlines, which, together with Lufthansa and Air Canada, is part of the transatlantic joint venture "A++", which Ita is also set to join that year. The Newark service—which would be in addition to existing flights to JFK—would likely start with one daily flight to enable connections with United’s network. In Asia, Singapore is almost certain to be added to Ita’s network, also because Lufthansa has a joint venture there with Singapore Airlines. Meanwhile, in Japan—where Ita already flies to Tokyo Haneda—the airline is aiming to launch flights to Osaka, one of the hubs of All Nippon Airways, itself also in a joint venture with Lufthansa. In Africa, Ita is looking at deploying Airbus A321neo aircraft—single-aisle planes capable of flying medium-haul distances—for expansion. Two destinations currently lead the shortlist: Abidjan (Ivory Coast) and Lagos (Nigeria). However, expanding the intercontinental network might also require reevaluating some existing routes. Flights to Dakar (Senegal) are currently underperforming, as are those to Jeddah (Saudi Arabia). On the other hand, the Riyadh connection appears to be doing well.  The reopening of Russian airspace to European carriers could pave the way for Ita to launch routes to Moscow, Beijing, Shanghai, and Hong Kong. A more stable geopolitical environment would also allow the Italian airline to resume some historic Alitalia routes, such as Tehran, Beirut, and Amman—routes that were profitable for the former national carrier for a period of time. For the winter season—when Italians typically travel to warmer destinations—Ita is considering launching so-called "counter-seasonal" routes to Zanzibar, Santo Domingo, and Havana. These destinations would heavily rely on vacation package sales, particularly through travel agencies. One of the most talked-about potential routes is Santiago de Chile, a destination that was historically part of Alitalia’s network. “It could be an option for the coming years,” Eberhart said on the sidelines of the IATA assembly. “But the city is quite far from Rome, and we have to study the yields there, because even if the aircraft is full, you need a certain yield to operate profitably”. Data obtained by Corriere shows that in 2019, Alitalia operated the Rome–Santiago route with nearly €67 million in revenue and almost 120,000 passengers, but the route incurred a loss of €16 million. lberberi@corriere.it

title: Mundys mulls majority stake in London City and Catania airports
content:
Trump attacca la Ue: «Nazioni decadenti guidate da leader deboli». E paragona Zelensky all'imprenditore del circo P. T. Barnum Salva questo articolo e leggilo quando vuoi. Il servizio è dedicato agli utenti registrati. Trovi tutti gli articoli salvati nella tua area personale nella sezione preferiti e sull'app Corriere News. The Italian holding company aims to expand its airport portfolio and is exploring the possibility of acquiring at least 50% of London City Airport. The sale process for Catania-Comiso begins. The issue of shares and pricing Holding company Mundys is evaluating making investments worth hundreds of millions of euros in London City and Catania/Comiso airports, three sources familiar with the matter told Corriere della Sera. The discussions are still at a "preliminary" stage, sources said. But these moves confirm the Italian holding company’s intention to monitor all possible infrastructure opportunities, including those overseas, such as airports in Panama. Two of the four shareholders managing London City's airport will sell their shares, which together amount to 50% of the total ownership. The Italian holding company (controlled by Edizione and Blackstone) is among the interested parties looking to acquire that stake. According to industry estimates, half of London City’s shares are valued between €700 million and €900 million. Mundys is already active in the sector, managing Rome’s airports (Fiumicino and Ciampino) through Aeroporti di Roma and operating the terminals in Nice, Cannes, and Saint-Tropez with Aéroports de la Côte d’Azur.     



 Inaugurated in 1987 by Queen Elizabeth II, London City Airport was designed to serve the financial district. However, the facility has significant operational constraints. It has only one 1,508-meter runway, accommodating specific single-aisle aircraft models such as ATRs, Airbus A220-100s, Embraers, and Fokkers. The airport operator has requested approval for Airbus A320neo aircraft—larger (180-194 seats) than the currently authorized models—which would enable the airport to reach a cap of 9 million passengers by 2031, up from today’s 6.5 million, with fewer overall flights. In 2024, London City recorded 3.57 million passengers, still below the 5.1 million of 2019, before the Covid pandemic. In 2023—the last year with available financial data—the airport reported an operating profit of £6.66 million and a revenue of £102.69 million.  In 2016, a consortium composed of Canadian firms Omers, Otpp, Aimco, and UK-based Wren House (each holding 25%) acquired London City Airport for £2 billion (€2.45 billion at the time). Atlantia, the predecessor of Mundys, also competed for the acquisition but deemed the price—44 times the airport’s EBITDA—too high, given that the industry standard is around 15 times. In 2024, Macquarie Asset Management made an offer for Otpp’s stakes in five European airports: London City, Brussels, Copenhagen, Bristol, and Birmingham.  Since the other London City shareholders did not bid for Otpp’s 25% stake, Macquarie is now free to buy that. In recent weeks, Omers and Aimco have enlisted Morgan Stanley to assess their options, including a potential sale, which sources indicate is expected "no earlier than Q4 2025." For now, Wren House has no intention of selling its stake. When contacted by Corriere, Omers and Mundys declined to comment, and no response was received from Aimco.  Mundys has long sought to expand its airport portfolio. In recent weeks, Italian representatives have been spotted in Panama engaging with the local government about the potential privatization of several airports, starting with the main Tocumen Airport. One of the most coveted airport assets in Europe remains Sac, the company managing Catania and Comiso airports, an asset Mundys has never hidden its interest in.  In recent days, the Sac shareholders’ board (with the Camera di Commercio del Sud Est Sicilia holding a 60.64% majority stake) approved the procedures to begin privatization. A call for expressions of interest is expected to be published soon, with a deadline by the end of May. According to two sources following the matter, Sac aims to receive binding offers by September/October, expecting bids in the range of €800 million to €1 billion. As for Mundys, the company intends to make its move once the economic conditions become clearer. lberberi@corriere.it

Mundys mulls majority stake in London City and Catania airports Trump attacca la Ue: «Nazioni decadenti guidate da leader deboli». E paragona Zelensky all'imprenditore del circo P. T. Barnum Salva questo articolo e leggilo quando vuoi. Il servizio è dedicato agli utenti registrati. Trovi tutti gli articoli salvati nella tua area personale nella sezione preferiti e sull'app Corriere News. The Italian holding company aims to expand its airport portfolio and is exploring the possibility of acquiring at least 50% of London City Airport. The sale process for Catania-Comiso begins. The issue of shares and pricing Holding company Mundys is evaluating making investments worth hundreds of millions of euros in London City and Catania/Comiso airports, three sources familiar with the matter told Corriere della Sera. The discussions are still at a "preliminary" stage, sources said. But these moves confirm the Italian holding company’s intention to monitor all possible infrastructure opportunities, including those overseas, such as airports in Panama. Two of the four shareholders managing London City's airport will sell their shares, which together amount to 50% of the total ownership. The Italian holding company (controlled by Edizione and Blackstone) is among the interested parties looking to acquire that stake. According to industry estimates, half of London City’s shares are valued between €700 million and €900 million. Mundys is already active in the sector, managing Rome’s airports (Fiumicino and Ciampino) through Aeroporti di Roma and operating the terminals in Nice, Cannes, and Saint-Tropez with Aéroports de la Côte d’Azur.     



 Inaugurated in 1987 by Queen Elizabeth II, London City Airport was designed to serve the financial district. However, the facility has significant operational constraints. It has only one 1,508-meter runway, accommodating specific single-aisle aircraft models such as ATRs, Airbus A220-100s, Embraers, and Fokkers. The airport operator has requested approval for Airbus A320neo aircraft—larger (180-194 seats) than the currently authorized models—which would enable the airport to reach a cap of 9 million passengers by 2031, up from today’s 6.5 million, with fewer overall flights. In 2024, London City recorded 3.57 million passengers, still below the 5.1 million of 2019, before the Covid pandemic. In 2023—the last year with available financial data—the airport reported an operating profit of £6.66 million and a revenue of £102.69 million.  In 2016, a consortium composed of Canadian firms Omers, Otpp, Aimco, and UK-based Wren House (each holding 25%) acquired London City Airport for £2 billion (€2.45 billion at the time). Atlantia, the predecessor of Mundys, also competed for the acquisition but deemed the price—44 times the airport’s EBITDA—too high, given that the industry standard is around 15 times. In 2024, Macquarie Asset Management made an offer for Otpp’s stakes in five European airports: London City, Brussels, Copenhagen, Bristol, and Birmingham.  Since the other London City shareholders did not bid for Otpp’s 25% stake, Macquarie is now free to buy that. In recent weeks, Omers and Aimco have enlisted Morgan Stanley to assess their options, including a potential sale, which sources indicate is expected "no earlier than Q4 2025." For now, Wren House has no intention of selling its stake. When contacted by Corriere, Omers and Mundys declined to comment, and no response was received from Aimco.  Mundys has long sought to expand its airport portfolio. In recent weeks, Italian representatives have been spotted in Panama engaging with the local government about the potential privatization of several airports, starting with the main Tocumen Airport. One of the most coveted airport assets in Europe remains Sac, the company managing Catania and Comiso airports, an asset Mundys has never hidden its interest in.  In recent days, the Sac shareholders’ board (with the Camera di Commercio del Sud Est Sicilia holding a 60.64% majority stake) approved the procedures to begin privatization. A call for expressions of interest is expected to be published soon, with a deadline by the end of May. According to two sources following the matter, Sac aims to receive binding offers by September/October, expecting bids in the range of €800 million to €1 billion. As for Mundys, the company intends to make its move once the economic conditions become clearer. lberberi@corriere.it
